coredata.dc:title: Citric acid-derived carbon dots as excellent cysteine oxidase mimics for cysteine sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Zhen
Zeng, Qi
Deng, Qian
Yao, Wensong
Deng, Haohua
Lin, Xinhua
Chen, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been receiving increasing attention in the field of nanozymes owing to their excellent physical and chemical properties and low toxicity. However, most reported CDs are doped with metals and nitrogen elements. Few studies have investigated the mimic enzyme activity of undoped CDs. In this study, we developed a facile one-pot hydrothermal strategy to synthesize CDs using citric acid as a single carbon source (denoted as CA-CDs). Further, we found that CA-CDs possessed excellent cysteine (Cys) oxidase-mimicking activity. The CA-CDs showed typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics and better affinity for Cys than natural Cys oxidase. Based on the Cys oxidase-mimicking activity of CA-CDs, a simple and sensitive “turn-on” mode fluorescent sensor for the detection of Cys was constructed. The proposed method had a detection limit of 0.036 µM with excellent selectivity and stability and has been successfully applied to the detection of Cys in serum samples. More importantly, this research not only expands the nanozyme field of CA-CDs without doping but also provides a research foundation for the synthesis and design of carbon-based nanozymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteine oxidase
Carbon dots
Enzyme mimics
Cysteine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cys sensing in real serum samples
Mechanism of oxidase-mimicking activity of CA-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CA-CDs. Insert photograph: Size distributions corresponding to the TEM images. (B) HRTEM image of CA-CDs. (C) UV–vis spectra of CA and CA-CDs. (D) FTIR spectra of CA and CA-CDs.

(A) Emission spectra of TA, Cys, CA-CDs, Cys+TA, CA-CDs+Cys, and CA-CDs+Cys+TA. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CA-CDs+Cys+TA system in the presence of air and N2 (C) Production of cystine; (D) Generation of H2O2.

(A) Emission spectra of the reaction system with different Cys concentrations. The concentration of Cys from a to h is: 0.5 µM, 1 µM, 5 µM, 10 µM, 20 µM, 30 µM, 40 µM, 50 µM. (B) Calibration plot for Cys detection. (C) Selectivity of the method. Comparison of fluorescence intensity of different interfering substances and Cys. (a–x: glucose, fructose, maltose, NaCl, oxalic acid, L-arginine, L-isoleucine, L-leucine, L-lysine, L-serine, L-methionine, L-alanine, L-valine, L-tryptophan, D-threonine, L-histidine, Proline, Sarcosine, CA, uric acid, glycine, Glutathione, cystine, and Cys.). (D) Time stability for Cys oxidase-like activities of CA-CDs.
Schematic of the Cys oxidase-like activity of CA-CDs.
Steady-state kinetic parameters of natural Cys oxidase and CA-CDs.
Detection of Cys in spiked human serum samples (n = 3).
Comparison of this work and Cys assay kit for the determination of serum Cys content (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Citric acid-derived carbon dots as excellent cysteine oxidase mimics for cysteine sensing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Faculty of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been receiving increasing attention in the field of nanozymes owing to their excellent physical and chemical properties and low toxicity. However, most reported CDs are doped with metals and nitrogen elements. Few studies have investigated the mimic enzyme activity of undoped CDs. In this study, we developed a facile one-pot hydrothermal strategy to synthesize CDs using citric acid as a single carbon source (denoted as CA-CDs). Further, we found that CA-CDs possessed excellent cysteine (Cys) oxidase-mimicking activity. The CA-CDs showed typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics and better affinity for Cys than natural Cys oxidase. Based on the Cys oxidase-mimicking activity of CA-CDs, a simple and sensitive “turn-on” mode fluorescent sensor for the detection of Cys was constructed. The proposed method had a detection limit of 0.036 µM with excellent selectivity and stability and has been successfully applied to the detection of Cys in serum samples. More importantly, this research not only expands the nanozyme field of CA-CDs without doping but also provides a research foundation for the synthesis and design of carbon-based nanozymes.

ce:section.ce:para: As biocatalysts, natural enzymes have advantage of high catalytic efficiency and excellent specificity; however, their practical feasibility remains unsatisfactory because of their low stability and high cost [1,2]. Therefore, various artificial enzymes have been developed as promising substitutes for natural enzymes. Carbon dots (CDs), a carbon-based nanomaterial, can be prepared from natural carbon sources and are ideal candidates for nanozymes, which have the advantages of small size, adequate water solubility, high photostability and biocompatibility, and low toxicity [3,4]; further, they have been widely used in cancer treatment [5], pollutant degradation [6], and molecular detection [7].

Doping is an effective method to fabricate CDs for obtaining more nanozymes. In most cases, the carbon nanoenzyme materials were prepared via metal doping and nitrogen doping. Zhao et al. synthesized Mo-doped carbon quantum dots (Mo-CDs) using ethanol and MoS2 as raw materials, which have peroxidase activity, and constructed a colorimetric sensor by catalyzing 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce oxidation products in the presence of H2O2 [8]. Fan et al. used tartaric acid and 3-aminophenol as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, to prepare nitrogen-doped CDs with oxidase-mimetic activity using a hydrothermal method [9]. Zhuo et al. synthesized manganese-doped CDs that can quickly oxidize colorless TMB into a blue oxidation product in the absence of H2O2, thereby exhibiting a catalytic activity similar to that of oxidase [10]. In our previous study, atomically dispersed Cu-N-C site-anchored carbon material was prepared using nitrogen on chlorophyll as the anchor site of copper atoms and citric acid (CA) as the carbon source, which exhibited excellent peroxidase mimetic properties [11].

Cysteine (Cys) is an essential amino acid present and plays a significant role in various cellular functions, such as protein synthesis and detoxification [12,13] in the human body. In addition, it is also associated with many pathological conditions, including Parkinson's disease, autoimmune deficiency syndrome and Alzheimer's disease [14,15]. Cys oxidase is distributed in plants, animal tissues, and bacteria. Cys oxidase affects Cys-related metabolic reactions and regulates the stability. However, the activity of natural Cys oxidase is easily influenced by environmental conditions. Therefore, developing Cys oxidase mimics with low cost, high activity, and good stability is of significant importance.

In this study, we used CA as the single carbon source to synthesize undoped CDs (denoted as CA-CDs) with excellent Cys oxidase-mimicking activity. The affinity of CA-CDs for Cys is considerably higher than that of natural Cys oxidase. CA-CDs can catalyze the oxidation of Cys to generate cystine and H2O2 in the presence of oxygen. Fluorescent methods are sensitive for Cys sensing [16–18]. Based on the Cys oxidase-mimicking activity of CA-CDs, we developed a “turn-on” fluorescent method for Cys detection. The proposed method is sensitive, accurate and easy to operate. Furthermore, CA-CDs can be easily synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method.

CA, sodium acetate trihydrate, acetic acid, L-cysteine, boric acid, L-cystine, 3-mercapto-1-propanol, terephthalic acid, glucose, fructose, maltose, NaCl, oxalic acid, L-arginine, L-serine, L-leucine, L-isoleucine, L-methionine, L-lysine, L-alanine, L-valine, L-tryptophan, D-threonine, L-histidine, proline, sarcosine, uric acid, glycine, and glutathione were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. NaOH, HCl, NaCl, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), and o-phthalaldehyde were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis bags and catalase from bovine liver were bought from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Blood samples were from Beijing Solarbio Science and Technology Co., Ltd.

The morphologies of CA-CDs were detected via transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100F). Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were recorded via a cary-300 spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using an iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet Thermo, USA). The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were monitored by a Bruker EMX ESR spectrometer (Billerica, MA). A Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) was used to record the fluorescence spectrum. The elemental composition and chemical bond composition of the CA-CDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Scientific ESCALAB250) Monochromatized Al Kα radiation was used in the XPS. A Bruker 600 MHz Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectrometer was used to record 13C NMR spectra of the CA-CDs.

CA-CDs were synthesized via a solvothermal method. CA (0.3 g) was dissolved in water; subsequently, the solution was pyrolyzed at 200 °C for 12 h. The crude product was purified by dialysis and dried under high-vacuum conditions to obtain CA-CDs for further use.

In this study, purified CA-CDs (14.13 mg/mL) were used in the experiments. The kinetic measurements of the CA-CDs were performed by changing the Cys concentrations. The initial rate of fluorescence change at different Cys concentrations was calculated by considering Cys concentration and initial rate of fluorescence change as the abscissa and ordinate, respectively. The Michaelis constant, K m, can be obtained by fitting the Michaelis equation, 1/V = K m/v max (1/[S] + 1/K m), where V represents the speed of the enzymatic reaction; K m is the Michaelis constant; v max is the maximum reaction rate; and [S] represents the substrate concentration [19].

100 µL CA-CDs (14.13 mg/mL), 200 µL Cys with different concentrations, 500 µL terephthalic acid (TA; 80 mM) and 3.4 mL of 0.2 M acetate buffer (pH 5.0) were mixed. The mixed solution was further heated at 60 °C for 30 min. The fluorescence spectrum of the mixed solution was recorded via fluorescence spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cys sensing in real serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: For measuring Cys in blood, samples were pretreated by ultrafiltration at 7000 rpm for 30 min to eliminate the possible interference in fluorescence detection. The supernatant was diluted 30 times by 0.2 M acetate buffer (pH 5.0). Cys in the pretreated samples was determined according to the above mentioned processes for Cys detection. Recovery experiments were conducted by adding three different concentrations of Cys (100, 200, and 300 µM) to the real serum samples.

The morphology of CA-CDs was characterized by TEM. Spherical CA-CDs exhibited a uniform dispersion with an average particle size of 1.65 nm ( Fig. 1A). CA-CDs have a lattice spacing of 0.206 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite (Fig. 1B) [20]. The UV absorption of CA-CDs at 250–270 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated C = C units of the newly formed CDs, which is different from CA, and indicated that conjugated groups were formed during the hydrothermal reaction (Fig. 1C). To further understand the distribution of the functional groups of CA-CDs, the characteristic groups of CA-CDs were analyzed via FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1D). The absorption peaks of CA-CDs at 1398 cm−1 and 1633 cm−1 are attributed to the vibration peaks of C – H and C = C, respectively. The results demonstrate that C = C groups were formed on the surface of CA-CDs. Furthermore, the peaks ranging from 910 cm−1 to 665 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibration of the benzene ring = C – H group, confirmed the formation of benzene ring structures on the surface of CA-CDs [21].

CA-CDs were characterized by XPS to gain a deeper understanding of their elemental composition and molecular structure. As shown in Fig. S1, there are only two elements, C and O, in CA-CDs. The relative contents of C and O were 58.98% and 41.02%, respectively. The O1s peaks locating at 532.3 and 533.4 eV corresponded to the C = O and C – O groups, respectively. The C1s peaks with binding energies of 284.9, 286.2, and 289.2 eV are assigned to C = C/C – C, C – O, and C = O groups, respectively [22]. In the NMR-13C resonance of CA-CDs, the signal at 100–150 ppm belonged to the sp2 hybrid carbon atom of the olefin and aromatic, which proved the existence of C = C and aromatic structures in CA-CDs (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of oxidase-mimicking activity of CA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2A shows no fluorescence generated in TA, TA-CA-CDs, or TA-Cys system. Interestingly, hydroxyterephthalate (TA-OH) with high fluorescence signal at 430 nm under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm [23,24] was observed in the TA-Cys-CA-CDs system. Therefore, it is assumed that CA-CDs possess Cys oxidase-mimetic activity. In the presence of oxygen, CA-CDs can catalyze the oxidation of Cys to produce cystine and H2O2.

The fluorescence intensity of the TA-Cys-CA-CDs system decreased drastically when the oxygen content in the solution decreased to 2.57 mg/L (Fig. 2B). This indicates that dissolved oxygen participates in the catalytic reaction. To verify the generation of cystine in the reaction process, we used o-phthalaldehyde (OPA) as a derivation agent for cystine. Cystine-OPA derivatives exhibited UV absorption peaks at λ = 335 nm. When CA-CDs were added to the Cys-OPA mixture, the UV absorption peaks at λ = 335 nm increased, which indicates the oxidation of Cys to cystine in the system (Fig. 2C) [25]. The fluorescence intensity was significantly inhibited by catalase (Fig. 2D), proving that CA-CDs catalyzed the production of H2O2 during the oxidation of Cys.

It has been reported that Cys can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to •OH [26,27]. To further study the reaction mechanism of the CA-CDs-Cys-TA fluorescence system, 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolin-N-oxide (DMPO) was used as the •OH capture agent. The ESR signal of the captured product, DMPO-OH, was detected in the CA-CD-Cys-TA system (Fig. S3). However, the DMPO-OH signal cannot be effectively detected in a pure CA-CDs solution. This result verified the generation of •OH in the CA-CDs-Cys-TA system. Moreover, we found that TA could be changed to TA-OH with strong fluorescence in the presence of Cys-H2O2 (Fig. S4). Based on the above results, we can confirm that CA-CDs exhibit Cys oxidase-like activity, thus catalyzing Cys to generate cystine and H2O2 in the presence of O2. Subsequently, Cys accelerated the decomposition of H2O2 into OH• radicals. Finally, the OH• radicals were captured by TA to produce TA-OH ( Scheme 1).

In this study, the effects of pH, temperature, TA concentration, and reaction time on the oxidase-mimicking catalytic activities of CA-CDs were investigated (Fig. S5). The results show that the highest oxidase-mimicking activities were obtained at a pH value of 5.0 in a 0.2 M acetate buffer solution (at 60 ℃). The final concentration of TA is 9.5 mM. The optimized reaction time is 30 min. To better understand the Cys oxidase-like activity of CA-CDs, we studied the steady-state kinetic characteristics of the CA-CDs-TA-Cys system. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the apparent steady-state kinetic parameters of the reaction system were determined. Fig. S6 showed K m of CA-CDs is 0.024 mM, which is much lower than that of natural Cys oxidase from either the bacterial or the adult rat brain ( Table 1). These results suggest that the affinity of CDs for Cys is much higher than that of natural Cys oxidase.

Therefore, based on the Cys oxidase-mimicking catalytic activities of CA-CDs, we constructed a simple and sensitive “turn-on” fluorescent sensor to detect Cys ( Fig. 3A and B). Within the linear range, the fluorescence images corresponding to different Cys concentrations can be seen in Fig. 3A. The corresponding linear equation for Cys detection was F−F0 = 15.74 CCys− 17.77 (µM, R2 = 0.9960), and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.036 µM (S/N = 3). Compared with other Cys detection methods reported in previous studies [30–40], the analytical performance of the proposed “turn-on” method for Cys has a wider linear range, better sensitivity, and relatively lower detection limit (Table S1). Subsequently, we assessed the selectivity of the method. Fig. 3C shows that the interferences with concentrations 10 or 100 times higher than that of Cys have no effect on the Cys detection, which demonstrates the high selectivity of the present method.

The robustness investigation showed that CA-CDs retained 94% of their relative activity after exposure to extreme temperatures (20–80 °C) and maintained over 88% of their relative activity at pH values ranging from to 2–9 (Fig. S7). The relative catalytic activity of CA-CDs remained almost unchanged after storage at room temperature for 2 weeks (Fig. 3D).

The proposed method has been applied for Cys detection in human serum to evaluate the analytical feasibility of the sensor in practical applications. Different amounts of Cys were spiked into human serum for recovery tests. The recoveries were in the range of 89.04–115.01% ( Table 2), and RSDs were within 2.22%. The determination results of Cys concentration in human serum samples by the proposed method and the commercial Cys detection kit have been compared ( Table 3). The statistical results of F-test and t-test show that there is no significant difference at the 95% confidence level. The results indicate that the proposed Cys sensor is suitable for real-sample analysis.

We demonstrated that CA-derived undoped CA-CDs had excellent Cys oxidase-mimicking activity. The CA-CDs exhibited a significantly higher affinity for Cys than natural Cys oxidase. CA-CDs can catalyze the oxidation of Cys to generate cystine and H2O2 in the presence of oxygen. Cys accelerated the decomposition of H2O2 into OH• radicals. OH• oxidizes TA to produce fluorescent TA-OH. Based on the tandem reaction, a sensitive turn-on fluorescent method for Cys detection was established. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of undoped CA-CDs with excellent Cys oxidase-mimicking activity, which broadens the scope of the enzyme-like activity of undoped CA-CDs. Furthermore, this work provides a research foundation for the synthesis and design of carbon-based nanozymes.

Zhen Lin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing, Reviewing and Funding acquisition. Qi Zeng: Data curation, Writing and Editing the manusript. Qian Deng: Data curation and Editing the manusript. Wensong Yao: Sample analyses, Editing the manusript. Haohua Deng: Data curation, Editing the manusript. Xinhua Lin: Reviewing and Editing the manusript. Wei Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Reviewing and Editing the manusript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2020J01629, 2020J01437, 2016J06019), Program for Young Top-notch Innovative Talents of Fujian Province of China, The Talent Training Project of Fujian Provincial Health and Family Planning Commission (2018-CX-39), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775023).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131563.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Catalytic principles from natural enzymes and translational design strategies for synthetic catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.-L. Li, T. Head-Gordon, Catalytic Principles from Natural Enzymes and Translational Design Strategies for Synthetic Catalysts, ACS. Central. Sci, 7(2021) 72–80.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanozyme: new horizons for responsive biomedical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Jiang, D. Ni, Z.T. Rosenkrans, P. Huang, X. Yan, W. Cai, Nanozyme: new horizons for responsive biomedical applications, Chem. Soc. Rev, 48(2019) 3683–3704.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in functionalized carbon dots toward the design of efficient materials for sensing and catalysis applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Dhenadhayalan, K.-C. Lin, T.A. Saleh, Recent Advances in Functionalized Carbon Dots toward the Design of Efficient Materials for Sensing and Catalysis Applications, Small, 16(2020) 1905767.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanozymes: enzymatic properties, catalytic mechanism, and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Sun, Y. Zhou, J. Ren, X. Qu, Carbon Nanozymes: Enzymatic Properties, Catalytic Mechanism, and Applications, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed, 57(2018) 9224–9237.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots confined in N-doped carbon as peroxidase-like nanozyme for detection of gastric cancer relevant D-amino acids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, W. Liu, P. Ni, C. Zhang, B. Wang, G. Duan, C. Chen, Y. Jiang, Y. Lu, Carbon dots confined in N-doped carbon as peroxidase-like nanozyme for detection of gastric cancer relevant D-amino acids, Chem. Eng. J, 428(2022) 131396.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient oxygen photosensitization of carbon dots: the role of nitrogen doping

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wu, R. Zhou, H. Chen, J. Zhang, P. Wu, Highly efficient oxygen photosensitization of carbon dots: the role of nitrogen doping, Nanoscale, 12(2020) 5543–5553.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of C-dots/Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposite as an efficient new nanozyme and its application for determination of H2O2 in nanomolar level

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Yousefinejad, H. Rasti, M. Hajebi, M. Kowsari, S. Sadravi, F. Honarasa, Design of C-dots/Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposite as an efficient new nanozyme and its application for determination of H2O2 in nanomolar level, Sens. Actuator B Chem, 247(2017) 691–696.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-activity Mo, S co-doped carbon quantum dot nanozyme-based cascade colorimetric biosensor for sensitive detection of cholesterol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhao, Z. Wu, G. Liu, H. Lu, Y. Gao, F. Liu, C. Wang, J. Cui, G. Lu, High-activity Mo, S co-doped carbon quantum dot nanozyme-based cascade colorimetric biosensor for sensitive detection of cholesterol, J. Mater. Chem. B, 7(2019) 7042–7051.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pyrrolic nitrogen dominated the carbon dot mimic oxidase activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, M. Zhang, K. Wei, Y. Zhao, H. Nie, Y. Ma, Y. Zhou, H. Huang, Y. Liu, M. Shao, Z. Kang, Pyrrolic nitrogen dominated the carbon dot mimic oxidase activity, Carbon, 179(2021) 692–700.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Manganese(II)-doped carbon dots as effective oxidase mimics for sensitive colorimetric determination of ascorbic acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhuo, J. Fang, M. Li, J. Wang, C. Zhu, J. Du, Manganese(II)-doped carbon dots as effective oxidase mimics for sensitive colorimetric determination of ascorbic acid, Microchim. Acta, 186(2019) 745.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile route for constructing Cu–N–C peroxidase mimics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Lin, L. Zheng, W. Yao, S. Liu, Y. Bu, Q. Zeng, X. Zhang, H. Deng, X. Lin, W. Chen, A facile route for constructing Cu–N–C peroxidase mimics, J. Mater. Chem. B, 8(2020) 8599–8606.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cysteine metabolism in neuronal redox homeostasis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.D. Paul, J.I. Sbodio, S.H. Snyder, Cysteine Metabolism in Neuronal Redox Homeostasis, Trends. Pharmacol. Sci, 39(2018) 513–524.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activity-based sensing for site-specific proteomic analysis of cysteine oxidation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Shi, K.S. Carroll, Activity-Based Sensing for Site-Specific Proteomic Analysis of Cysteine Oxidation, Accounts. Chem. Res, 53(2020) 20–31.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High peroxidase-mimicking activity of gold@platinum bimetallic nanoparticle-supported molybdenum disulfide nanohybrids for the selective colorimetric analysis of cysteine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wan, L. Wu, S. Su, D. Zhu, J. Chao, L. Wang, High peroxidase-mimicking activity of gold@platinum bimetallic nanoparticle-supported molybdenum disulfide nanohybrids for the selective colorimetric analysis of cysteine, Chem. Commun, 56(2020) 12351–12354.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Independent bi-reversible reactions and regulable FRET efficiency achieving real-time visualization of Cys metabolizing into SO2

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric, fast-responsive, and single-wavelength excited fluorescent probe for the discrimination of Cys and Hcy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Han, H. Zhang, X. Yue, J. Wang, L. Yang, B. Wang, X. Song, A Ratiometric, Fast-Responsive, and Single-Wavelength Excited Fluorescent Probe for the Discrimination of Cys and Hcy, Anal. Chem, 93(2021) 10934–10939.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydro-soluble NIR fluorescent probe with multiple sites and multiple excitations for distinguishing visualization of endogenous Cys/Hcy, and GSH

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A two-photon multi-emissive fluorescent probe for discrimination of Cys and Hcy/GSH via an aromatic substitution-rearrangement

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Luo, S. Zhang, Q. Meng, J. Zhou, R. Jin, X. Long, Y.P. Tang, H. Guo, A two-photon multi-emissive fluorescent probe for discrimination of Cys and Hcy/GSH via an aromatic substitution-rearrangement, Talanta, 224(2021) 121833.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of copper(II) oxide nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.-B. He, A.-L. Hu, Q.-Q. Zhuang, H.-P. Peng, H.-H. Deng, W. Chen, G.L. Hong, Ascorbate Oxidase Mimetic Activity of Copper(II) Oxide Nanoparticles, ChemBioChem, 21(2020) 978–984.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene oxide derived graphene quantum dots with different photoluminescence properties and peroxidase-like catalytic activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Tang, J. Liu, X. Yan, L. Kang, Graphene oxide derived graphene quantum dots with different photoluminescence properties and peroxidase-like catalytic activity, RSC Adv, 6(2016) 50609–50617.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile preparation of N-S co-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from graphite waste for efficient humidity sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jlassi, S. Mallick, A. Eribi, M.M. Chehimi, Z. Ahmad, F. Touati, I. Krupa, Facile preparation of N-S co-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from graphite waste for efficient humidity sensing, Sens. Actuator B Chem, 328(2021) 129058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of carbon nanodots with surface state-modulated fluorescence for highly sensitive and real-time detection of water in organic solvents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Ye, Y. Qin, P. Huang, A. Chen, F.-Y. Wu, Facile synthesis of carbon nanodots with surface state-modulated fluorescence for highly sensitive and real-time detection of water in organic solvents, Anal. Chim. Acta, 1034(2018) 144–152.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-cascade system based on cupric oxide nanoparticles as dual-functional enzyme mimics for ultrasensitive detection of silver ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. He, Y. Lu, X. Gao, P. Song, Z. Huang, S. Liu, Y. Liu, Self-Cascade System Based on Cupric Oxide Nanoparticles as Dual-Functional Enzyme Mimics for Ultrasensitive Detection of Silver Ions, Acs. Sustain Chem Eng, 6(2018) 12132–12139.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bifunctional gold nanoclusters enable ratiometric fluorescence nanosensing of hydrogen peroxide and glucose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wu, Y. Gao, J. Du, Bifunctional gold nanoclusters enable ratiometric fluorescence nanosensing of hydrogen peroxide and glucose, Talanta, 197(2019) 599–604.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-cascade reaction catalyzed by CuO nanoparticle-based dual-functional enzyme mimics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.-L. Hu, H.-H. Deng, X.-Q. Zheng, Y.-Y. Wu, X.-L. Lin, A.-L. Liu, X.H. Xia, H.P. Peng, W. Chen, G.L. Hong, Self-cascade reaction catalyzed by CuO nanoparticle-based dual-functional enzyme mimics, Biosens. Bioelectron, 97(2017) 21–25.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Properties of O2•- and OH• formed in TiO2 aqueous suspensions by photocatalytic reaction and the influence of H2O2 and some ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Hirakawa, Y. Nosaka, Properties of O2•- and OH• Formed in TiO2 Aqueous Suspensions by Photocatalytic Reaction and the Influence of H2O2 and Some Ions, Langmuir, 18(2002) 3247–3254.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Competitive inhibition of the enzyme-mimic activity of Gd-based nanorods toward highly specific colorimetric sensing of l-cysteine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Singh, P. Weerathunge, P.D. Liyanage, E. Mayes, R. Ramanathan, V. Bansal, Competitive Inhibition of the Enzyme-Mimic Activity of Gd-Based Nanorods toward Highly Specific Colorimetric Sensing of l-Cysteine, Langmuir, 33(2017) 10006–10015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: L-cysteine oxidase activity in the membrane of Neisseria meningitidis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.K. Yu I.W. DeVoe L-cysteine oxidase activity in the membrane of Neisseria meningitidis J. Bacteriol. 145 1981 280 287.
C.H. Misra, J.W. Olney, Cysteine oxidase in brain, Brain Res, 97(1975) 117–126.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrodes modified with bacteriophages and carbon nanofibres for cysteine detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Szot-Karpińska, A. Leśniewski, M. Jönsson-Niedziółka, F. Marken, J. Niedziółka-Jönsson, Electrodes modified with bacteriophages and carbon nanofibres for cysteine detection, Sens. Actuator B Chem, 287(2019) 78–85.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive electrochemical detection of L-cysteine at a screen-printed diamond electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Matsunaga, T. Kondo, I. Shitanda, Y. Hoshi, M. Itagaki, T. Tojo, M. Yuasa, Sensitive electrochemical detection of l-Cysteine at a screen-printed diamond electrode, Carbon, 173(2021) 395–402.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 3D pothole-rich hierarchical carbon framework-encapsulated Ni nanoparticles for highly selective nonenzymatic cysteine detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, L. Zhang, 3D pothole-rich hierarchical carbon framework-encapsulated Ni nanoparticles for highly selective nonenzymatic cysteine detection, Electrochim. Acta, 328(2019) 135126.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Using bioluminescence turn-on to detect cysteine in vitro and in vivo

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, L. Wang, Y. Zhao, F. Wang, J. Wu, G. Liang, Using Bioluminescence Turn-On To Detect Cysteine in Vitro and in Vivo, Anal. Chem, 90(2018) 4951–4954.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots for highly selective and sensitive fluorescent detection of Fe(III) ions and L-cysteine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wu J. Jiang X. Gu C. Tong Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots for highly selective and sensitive fluorescent detection of Fe(III) ions and L-cysteine Microchim. Acta 184 2017 2291 2298.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of cysteine and its application in cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yu, J. Yang, X. Xu, Y. Jiang, B. Wang, A novel fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of cysteine and its application in cell imaging, Sens. Actuator B Chem, 251(2017) 902–908.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-doped graphene quantum dots-decorated v2o5 nanosheet for fluorescence turn off–on detection of cysteine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.B. Ganganboina, A. Dutta Chowdhury, R.-a Doong, N-Doped Graphene Quantum Dots-Decorated V2O5 Nanosheet for Fluorescence Turn Off–On Detection of Cysteine, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 10(2018) 614–624.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent probe for simultaneous discrimination of GSH, Cys, and SO2 derivatives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Jia, L.-Y. Niu, Q.-Z. Yang, Fluorescent Probe for Simultaneous Discrimination of GSH, Cys, and SO2 Derivatives, Anal. Chem, 92(2020) 10800–10806.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An NIR ESIPT-based fluorescent probe with large stokes shift for specific detection of Cys and its bioimaging in cells and mice

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ren, F. Huo, Y. Zhang, S. Zhao, C. Yin, An NIR ESIPT-based fluorescent probe with large stokes shift for specific detection of Cys and its bioimaging in cells and mice, Sens. Actuator B Chem, 319(2020) 128248.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescent probe with dual acrylate sites for discrimination of different concentration ranges of cysteine in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xu, S. Si, Z. Zhang, H. Tan, T. Qin, Z. Wang, D. Wang, L. Wang, B. Liu, A fluorescent probe with dual acrylate sites for discrimination of different concentration ranges of cysteine in living cells, Anal. Chim. Acta, 1176(2021) 338763.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Developing a dual-site fluorescent probe for simultaneously discriminating Cys/Hcy and GSH, and visualizing metabolism of Cys to SO2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Chao, J. Zhao, Y. Zhang, F. Huo, C. Yin, Developing a dual-site fluorescent probe for simultaneously discriminating Cys/Hcy and GSH, and visualizing metabolism of Cys to SO2, Sens. Actuator B Chem, 346(2021) 130569.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhen Lin is currently an associate professor of analytical chemistry in Fujian Medical University. She received her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Tsinghua University in 2012. Her research interests cover analytical chemistry and chemical biosensors.
Qi Zeng is currently a postgraduate student in Fujian Medical University. Her research interests are analytical chemistry and chemical biosensors.
Qian Deng is currently a postgraduate student in Fujian Medical University. Her research interests are analytical chemistry and chemical biosensors.

Wensong Yao is currently a lecturer in Ningde Normal University. He received his Ph.D. degree from Fujian Medical University in 2019. His research interests include functional materials, analytical chemistry and chemical biosensors.

Haohua Deng is currently an associate professor of analytical chemistry in Fujian Medical University. He received his Ph.D. degree from Fujian Medical University in 2017. His research interests include functional materials, analytical chemistry and chemical biosensors.
Xinhua Lin is currently a professor of analytical chemistry in Fujian Medical University. His research interests are analytical chemistry and chemical biosensors.

Wei Chen is currently a professor of analytical chemistry in Fujian Medical University. He received his Ph.D. degree in chemical biology from Nanjing University in 2010. His research interests are functional materials, analytical chemistry and chemical biosensors.
